topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper felt like an accusation. A C-minus. Not a B. Not even a solid B-. I stared at the grade as if it were a venomous insect crawling across the page. Every shadow in my cramped dorm room seemed to shift, elongating into something watchful. I checked the lock on the door for the fifth time, the metallic click sounding deafening in the silence. Who had altered it? Someone with access. Maybe the professor was in league with the TA. I started cataloging every loose thread on my desk, convinced that a hidden message was embroidered into the fabric, a coded warning only I could decipher. My hands trembled as I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic digging into my palm. The world outside the window felt too bright, too exposed, like a spotlight aimed directly at my exposed vulnerability.

[story 2]

"This is impossible." The words caught in my throat, sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I reread the feedback, the terse critique about my lack of critical insight. It wasn't just a bad grade; it was a deliberate sabotage. I glanced over my shoulder repeatedly, catching glimpses of movement in the hallway—a fleeting silhouette, a door closing too quickly. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the ambient noise of the building. I began rearranging my books, stacking them in precise, defensive formations, creating barriers against whatever unseen threat was lurking just beyond my peripheral vision. I kept whispering to myself, a low, constant murmur, testing the air. Was that a footstep? Or was it just the settling of the old building? I pressed my back against the cold cinderblock wall, trying to merge with the texture, hoping that if I stopped moving, I might become invisible.

[story 3]

I traced the margin of the report with my index finger, the paper slick under my touch. The sheer audacity of the mark—it felt like a deliberate insult, a calculated slight against my entire effort. I couldn't shake the feeling that the very air in the lecture hall had been poisoned. Everyone else seemed so calm, so composed, and I was the anomaly, the one who had somehow stumbled into a trap. I kept scanning the faces of my classmates, searching for any flicker of shared knowledge, any sign that they were also aware of this discrepancy. When I looked up, the fluorescent lights above flickered, plunging the room into a momentary, sickly darkness before snapping back on. In that instant, I saw a shape in the corner of my eye, too still, too deliberate. I quickly shoved the paper into my bag, my breath hitching, and started walking toward the exit, moving fast, needing distance from the scrutiny of the room.
